does: This paper identifies and addresses a critical vulnerability in fine-tuned text-to-image diffusion models where safety alignment breaks down during adaptation. The authors demonstrate that standard fine-tuning procedures can inadvertently undo safety measures, causing models to relearn previously suppressed harmful concepts like nudity even when trained on benign datasets. They propose Modular LoRA, a method that trains Safety Low-Rank Adaptation modules separately from Fine-Tuning LoRA components and merges them during inference. Through experimental evaluation, they show this approach effectively prevents the re-emergence of harmful content while maintaining performance on new tasks, providing an immediate practical solution to the fine-tuning jailbreaking problem in generative vision models.
connects: This work extends the fine-tuning jailbreaking problem previously identified in large language models to the vision domain, specifically text-to-image diffusion models. It builds on existing research in safety alignment methods for generative models and low-rank adaptation techniques that have become standard for efficient model personalization. The paper responds to the broader challenge of maintaining safety properties during model adaptation, a concern that has grown as fine-tuning becomes increasingly common for domain-specific applications. It combines insights from adversarial robustness, constitutional AI, and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods to address how safety measures persist through adaptation procedures.
enables: This work opens several future directions for robust safety in adaptable generative models. It enables more reliable personalization and domain adaptation while maintaining safety guarantees, which is crucial as these models proliferate in real-world applications. The Modular LoRA approach could be extended to other safety-critical aspects beyond harmful content filtering and generalized to other types of fine-tuning procedures. The findings motivate research into fundamental understanding of how safety properties are encoded and preserved in large models, as well as development of more robust alignment techniques that remain stable under distribution shifts and model updates. This could eventually lead to modular safety architectures that scale to increasingly capable generative systems.
